questions.question: Is there a cohesive CMC development plan aligned with the clinical and regulatory strategy?
questions.rubric_tests: Presence, quality, and governance of a cross-functionally integrated CMC development plan. For biologics, includes cell line development timeline, process development strategy (upstream/downstream), analytical method platform, and comparability strategy for process changes across development stages.
questions.rationale: A strategic CMC plan is essential for biologics where process defines product. Process changes during development require comparability assessments, making forward planning even more critical than for small molecules. ICH Q5E, Q6B, and Q11 provide the framework.

questions.question: Are there clear links between CMC timelines and clinical/commercial milestones?
questions.rubric_tests: Alignment of CMC timelines with clinical phases and commercial readiness. For biologics, includes cell line development timelines, bioreactor scale-up milestones, process lock timelines relative to pivotal trials, and BLA/MAA filing readiness including process validation.
questions.rationale: For biologics, CMC timelines are typically longer and more complex than for small molecules due to cell line development (12-18 months), process scale-up, and process validation requirements. Misalignment between CMC and clinical timelines is a leading cause of programme delay.

questions.question: Are critical CMC risks identified and mitigated?
questions.rubric_tests: Proactive identification and mitigation of CMC risks. For biologics, includes cell line stability risks, viral contamination risk, process-related impurity control, post-translational modification variability, aggregation, and immunogenicity driven by product quality attributes.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific CMC risks include cell line genetic instability, viral contamination events (which can shut down manufacturing), aggregation driving immunogenicity, and the fundamental principle that "the process is the product" — meaning process changes can alter clinical outcomes. ICH Q5A, Q5B, Q5C, Q5E, Q9.

questions.question: Is the upstream cell culture process (fermentation/bioreactor) defined, optimised, and scalable?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the upstream process (cell culture conditions, media composition, feed strategy, bioreactor mode, harvest criteria) is defined, optimised for product quality and yield, and demonstrated to be scalable from bench to pilot to commercial scale. Assessment of process parameters (temperature, pH, DO, feed timing) and their impact on CQAs.
questions.rationale: The upstream process is the foundational manufacturing step for biologics and directly determines product yield, quality attributes (glycosylation, charge variants, aggregation), and process economics. Poorly optimised upstream processes lead to low yields, inconsistent product quality, and commercial non-viability. ICH Q5B, Q11.

questions.question: Is the downstream purification process robust and capable of achieving required purity?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the downstream purification process (Protein A capture, ion exchange, hydrophobic interaction, viral inactivation/filtration steps) achieves target purity levels for process-related impurities (HCP, DNA, Protein A leachate) and product-related impurities (aggregates, fragments, charge variants). Assessment of step yields, resin lifetime, and scalability.
questions.rationale: Downstream purification defines the purity profile of the biologic drug substance. Inadequate clearance of host cell proteins (HCP), residual DNA, or Protein A can cause immunogenicity, safety risks, or regulatory rejection. Process-related impurity clearance must be demonstrated and validated. ICH Q6B.

questions.question: Are process-related and product-related impurities identified, characterised, and controlled?
questions.rubric_tests: Adequacy of impurity identification and control strategy. For biologics, process-related impurities include host cell proteins (HCP), host cell DNA, Protein A leachate, endotoxin, and residual media components. Product-related impurities include aggregates, fragments, deamidation products, oxidised variants, charge variants, and glycosylation variants. Assessment of whether impurity specifications are justified and phase-appropriate.
questions.rationale: Biologics have a fundamentally different impurity profile from small molecules. HCP, DNA, and endotoxin are process-related impurities unique to cell-based manufacturing. Product-related impurities (aggregates, fragments, charge variants) are inherent to protein therapeutics and must be controlled because they can affect safety (immunogenicity from aggregates), efficacy (fragments lacking binding), and stability. ICH Q6B, Q5E.

questions.question: Are DS release and stability specifications justified and aligned with phase?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether DS specifications are scientifically justified and phase-appropriate. For biologics, specifications must cover identity (peptide mapping, intact mass), purity (SEC for aggregates, CE-SDS for fragments, IEX/iCIEF for charge variants), potency (cell-based or binding assay), glycosylation profile, process-related impurities (HCP, DNA, endotoxin), and general tests (pH, appearance, osmolality).
questions.rationale: Biologic DS specifications are more complex than SM specifications due to the heterogeneous nature of proteins. Specifications must evolve with process understanding and data accumulation. ICH Q6B provides the framework for setting biologic specifications, which differs fundamentally from ICH Q6A for small molecules.

questions.question: Are DS materials sourced from GMP-qualified manufacturers?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether cell banks, raw materials, media components, chromatography resins, and the final DS are procured from qualified suppliers. For biologics, includes qualification of animal-derived raw materials (bovine serum, trypsin) or documented animal-free sourcing, and TSE/BSE risk assessment.
questions.rationale: Sourcing from GMP-qualified manufacturers ensures consistency and regulatory compliance. For biologics, animal-derived raw materials introduce TSE/BSE risks and are increasingly replaced with animal-free alternatives. Regulatory agencies require full traceability of biological raw materials. ICH Q5A, Q7.

questions.question: Are dual suppliers available or planned for critical materials?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether secondary sources are secured for critical materials, resins, single-use components, and the DS itself to ensure supply continuity.
questions.rationale: Single-source dependency for biologics is particularly risky due to the complexity and long lead times of biologic manufacturing. Loss of a critical resin or single-use component supplier can halt production for months.

questions.question: Is the formulation suitable for the target population and indication?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the DP formulation (buffer composition, pH, tonicity, surfactant, stabilisers) and presentation (liquid vs. lyophilised, vial vs. prefilled syringe vs. autoinjector) are designed for the intended patient population and clinical context. For biologics, includes high-concentration formulation challenges (viscosity, aggregation, injection volume) for SC administration.
questions.rationale: Biologic formulation must maintain protein stability (prevent aggregation, deamidation, oxidation) while being suitable for the route of administration. High-concentration SC formulations (often >100 mg/mL) present unique challenges around viscosity, syringeability, and injection site reactions. Formulation directly impacts immunogenicity risk. ICH Q5C, Q8.

questions.question: Is the formulation stable, scalable, and compatible with packaging and delivery devices?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the formulation maintains stability, is reproducible at scale, and is compatible with the container closure system and delivery device (prefilled syringe, autoinjector, on-body injector, IV bag). For biologics, includes protein-surface adsorption, silicone oil interaction, leachables/extractables from elastomeric closures, and device functionality testing.
questions.rationale: Biologics are sensitive to surface interactions (silicone oil from syringes, tungsten from syringe tips, leachables from stoppers) that can cause aggregation and sub-visible particle formation. These particles can drive immunogenicity. Device compatibility testing is critical for combination product regulatory pathways. ICH Q5C, FDA Combination Product guidance.

questions.question: Are all excipients pharmacopeial, justified, and qualified for parenteral use?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether excipients meet pharmacopeial standards and are justified for parenteral administration. For biologics, includes justification of buffer system, surfactant (polysorbate 80/20 degradation risks), stabiliser (sucrose, trehalose), tonicity agent, and assessment of novel excipients requiring additional safety data.
questions.rationale: Biologics are predominantly parenteral products, imposing stricter excipient requirements than oral SM formulations. Polysorbate degradation (a common biologic surfactant) can generate free fatty acids and sub-visible particles. Novel excipients for biologics may require separate safety data packages.

questions.question: Is the process reproducible and capable at commercial scale?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the DP manufacturing process (compounding, sterile filtration, filling, lyophilisation if applicable, inspection, labelling) is consistent and capable. For biologics, includes aseptic processing validation, fill-finish in isolator/RABS, lyophilisation cycle development, and particulate/sub-visible particle control.
questions.rationale: Biologic DP manufacturing is predominantly aseptic fill-finish, which introduces sterility assurance, particulate control, and container closure integrity challenges beyond typical SM manufacturing. Lyophilisation (if used) adds cycle development complexity. ICH Q5C, Annex 1.

questions.question: Are critical process parameters (CPPs) and critical quality attributes (CQAs) identified?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CPPs and CQAs are defined for the biologic DP. For biologics, CQAs include potency (biological activity), purity (aggregates, fragments, charge variants), identity, glycosylation profile, particulates (sub-visible and visible), sterility, endotoxin, container closure integrity, and for lyophilised products, reconstitution time and cake appearance.
questions.rationale: Biologic CQAs are fundamentally different from SM CQAs. Potency is measured by biological assays (cell-based or binding), not chemical purity. Aggregation, particulates, and glycosylation are CQAs unique to biologics that directly impact safety (immunogenicity) and efficacy. ICH Q5E, Q6B, Q8.

questions.question: Is the container closure system compatible, protective, and qualified — including delivery device if applicable?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the CCS is fit-for-purpose for a biologic, including protein-surface compatibility, extractables/leachables assessment, container closure integrity testing, and for combination products (prefilled syringe, autoinjector), device human factors engineering and regulatory pathway (FDA combination product, EU MDR).
questions.rationale: Biologic products in prefilled syringes or autoinjectors are combination products requiring dual regulatory review (drug + device). Human factors engineering, device reliability, and dose accuracy testing are additional requirements beyond standard CCS qualification. This is a significant regulatory and development complexity unique to biologic DP.

questions.question: Are analytical methods fully validated (or verified for early phase) for biologic-specific attributes?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether analytical methods for biologic DS and DP are appropriately validated. For biologics, includes potency assays (cell-based bioassays, binding assays), purity methods (SEC, CE-SDS, IEX/iCIEF), identity methods (peptide mapping, intact mass), glycosylation analysis (HILIC, CE-LIF), HCP ELISA, residual DNA assays, endotoxin/LAL, and sub-visible particle characterisation (MFI, FlowCAM).
questions.rationale: Biologic analytical methods are fundamentally different from SM methods. Potency is measured by bioassays, not chemical assays. Purity requires multiple orthogonal methods (SEC for aggregates, CE-SDS for fragments, charge variants by IEX). Glycosylation analysis, HCP, and DNA clearance assays have no SM equivalent. ICH Q2(R2), Q6B.

questions.question: Are stability-indicating methods available for biologic degradation pathways?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether analytical methods can detect biologic-specific degradation: aggregation (SEC, DLS, SV-AUC), fragmentation (CE-SDS), deamidation and isomerisation (peptide mapping, IEX), oxidation (RP-HPLC, peptide mapping), glycation, disulphide scrambling, and particulate formation. Assessment of forced degradation studies establishing degradation pathways.
questions.rationale: Biologics degrade through protein-specific pathways (aggregation, deamidation, oxidation, fragmentation) entirely different from SM chemical degradation. Stability-indicating methods must be demonstrated to detect each relevant degradation pathway. Forced degradation studies for biologics must include thermal, oxidative, light, freeze-thaw, and agitation stress. ICH Q5C.

questions.question: Are methods suitable for release, in-process control (IPC), characterisation, and comparability?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether methods cover all intended uses. For biologics, comparability assessment (ICH Q5E) following process changes is a critical additional use case requiring sensitive, qualified methods. IPC methods for biologics include harvest turbidity, Protein A eluate pool pH, viral inactivation hold time/temperature, and nanofiltration integrity.
questions.rationale: For biologics, comparability assessment after process changes is a regulatory requirement (ICH Q5E) that demands highly sensitive analytical methods capable of detecting subtle changes in product quality attributes. This is an additional method use case beyond standard release/IPC that does not exist for small molecules.

questions.question: Is method transfer to contract labs complete or underway?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether analytical methods are transferred to CDMOs/CTLs. For biologics, includes transfer of complex bioassays (cell-based potency), glycosylation methods, and HCP ELISA — methods that are particularly challenging to transfer due to biological variability and operator dependence.
questions.rationale: Biologic analytical methods — particularly cell-based potency assays — are significantly more difficult to transfer than SM HPLC methods. Transfer of bioassays requires careful attention to cell bank management, assay plate layout, reader calibration, and reference standard qualification at the receiving site.

questions.question: Is there sufficient stability data under ICH conditions to support the current phase?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether stability data quantity and duration are adequate. For biologics, stability assessment per ICH Q5C (not just Q1A) including storage at 2-8°C (typical), accelerated at 25°C, and stress conditions. Includes assessment of biologic-specific stability attributes (aggregation, potency, charge variants, glycosylation, particulates).
questions.rationale: Biologic stability testing follows ICH Q5C, which differs from Q1A in storage conditions (most biologics are refrigerated), study design, and attributes tested. Biologic-specific degradation pathways must be monitored. Stability data directly determines shelf-life, which is often a competitive differentiator (longer shelf-life enables broader distribution).

questions.question: Are the data adequate to support retest and shelf-life claims?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether stability data support shelf-life assignment. For biologics, includes assessment of potency trends, aggregation progression, particulate formation over time, and whether statistical analysis supports the proposed shelf-life at the proposed storage temperature.
questions.rationale: Biologic shelf-lives are typically shorter than SM shelf-lives (often 18-36 months at 2-8°C) and are driven by protein-specific degradation. Aggregation progression is often the shelf-life-limiting attribute.

questions.question: Is stability tracked across both DS and DP with relevant packaging?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether stability programmes cover DS (frozen bulk or liquid bulk), DP in commercial packaging, and in-use stability. For biologics, includes hold-time studies for intermediates (harvest, purification pools, compounded bulk), freeze-thaw studies for frozen DS, and in-use stability for multi-dose presentations.
questions.rationale: Biologics have multiple intermediate hold points (harvest, Protein A pool, viral inactivation hold, compounded bulk) each requiring hold-time/temperature qualification. Frozen DS requires freeze-thaw stability. These intermediate stability considerations are unique to biologic manufacturing.

questions.question: Is there a clear plan to extend shelf life with ongoing studies?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a plan exists for shelf-life extension. For biologics, includes strategy for generating real-time data at commercial scale, and assessment of whether process changes during development require new stability campaigns or comparability-based shelf-life bridging.
questions.rationale: Shelf-life extension for biologics often requires new stability campaigns after process changes (per ICH Q5E comparability). Each significant process change may reset the stability clock, making forward planning essential.

questions.question: Are GMP-compliant manufacturers (CMOs or internal) qualified and audited?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether manufacturing sites are GMP-compliant and audited. For biologics, includes assessment of biologic-specific GMP capabilities (cell culture, purification, aseptic fill-finish, lyophilisation) and whether the manufacturer has relevant regulatory track record (FDA/EMA-inspected for biologics).
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing requires specialised facilities and capabilities (bioreactors, chromatography suites, cleanrooms, aseptic fill-finish) that are distinct from SM manufacturing. Not all CMOs have biologic capability. Regulatory inspection history for biologics is a key qualification criterion.

questions.question: Is the supply chain secure and robust?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the supply chain has sufficient redundancy and risk mitigation. For biologics, includes assessment of single-use component supply, resin supply, cell bank inventory, and cold chain requirements (2-8°C or -20°C/-70°C for DS).
questions.rationale: Biologic supply chains are more fragile than SM supply chains due to dependence on specialised raw materials (resins, single-use assemblies, cell culture media), cold chain requirements, and the long manufacturing cycle times (often 3-6 months seed-to-release).

questions.question: Is the manufacturing history (batch records, deviations, OOS) clean and well-documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether manufacturing history is thorough and traceable. For biologics, includes documentation of any contamination events (viral, microbial), cell culture deviations, and comparability data from process changes.
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing history is scrutinised intensively at BLA review and pre-approval inspection. Contamination events are particularly serious for cell-based manufacturing and must be fully documented with root cause analysis and corrective actions.

questions.question: Are there supply agreements in place or in negotiation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether formal supply agreements are in place with CDMOs.
questions.rationale: Well-structured supply agreements are essential. For biologics, agreements must address specialised capacity reservation, technology transfer terms, IP protections for cell lines and processes, and contamination liability.

questions.question: Is process development at the appropriate stage for the clinical phase?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether process development maturity matches the clinical stage. For biologics, includes upstream process (cell culture), downstream process (purification), and overall process lock strategy. Assessment of whether the process intended for pivotal trials is representative of the commercial process.
questions.rationale: For biologics, "process defines product" — process changes can alter the product and require comparability assessment. The timing of process lock relative to pivotal trials is a critical strategic decision. Locking too early limits optimisation; locking too late creates comparability risk.

questions.question: Are scale-up risks identified and addressed (yield, product quality shifts, CQA changes)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether scale-up risks are assessed. For biologics, includes risks of cell culture performance changes at larger bioreactor volumes (mixing, mass transfer, shear), chromatography scale-up challenges (packing quality, residence time), and potential CQA shifts (glycosylation, charge variants, aggregation) at commercial scale.
questions.rationale: Biologic scale-up introduces unique risks: cell culture performance often changes with bioreactor scale due to fluid dynamics differences; chromatography performance varies with column packing quality; and CQAs (particularly glycosylation) can shift with process scale. These must be prospectively risk-assessed.

questions.question: Is tech transfer completed or planned (site-to-site)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether technology transfer is planned or executed. For biologics, includes transfer of cell bank management, upstream process (cell culture), downstream process (purification), viral safety procedures, and analytical methods — each requiring specific transfer protocols.
questions.rationale: Biologic tech transfer is significantly more complex than SM transfer because it involves living systems (cell banks, cell culture) and multi-step purification processes. Each step requires independent transfer validation.

questions.question: Is a Quality by Design (QbD) or control strategy defined?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a QbD framework or control strategy is in place. For biologics, includes design space definition for upstream (cell culture parameters) and downstream (purification parameters), and linkage between process parameters and biologic-specific CQAs (glycosylation, aggregation, charge variants, HCP clearance).
questions.rationale: QbD for biologics must address the relationship between process parameters and protein quality attributes — a fundamentally more complex relationship than for SM manufacturing. Understanding how cell culture conditions affect glycosylation, for example, is essential for process control. ICH Q8, Q11.

questions.question: Are the Module 3 CTD sections (or equivalents) complete or in progress?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CTD Module 3 is prepared. For biologics, Module 3 structure differs from SM: 3.2.S covers the biologic DS (cell substrate, manufacturing process, characterisation, reference standards, CCS), and must include cell line history, upstream/downstream process descriptions, viral safety data, and comparability data. 3.2.P covers the DP including device information if a combination product.
questions.rationale: Module 3 for biologics is substantially more complex than for SM, requiring detailed cell substrate information, viral safety data, and comparability assessments that have no SM equivalent. BLA Module 3 deficiencies are a leading cause of FDA Complete Response Letters for biologics.

questions.question: Are recent regulatory interactions documented (CMC questions, responses)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CMC-related regulatory interactions are documented.
questions.rationale: Effective documentation of regulatory interactions is critical for maintaining alignment with agency expectations.

questions.question: Are all facilities appropriately registered and inspection-ready?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether facilities are registered and inspection-ready. For biologics, includes assessment of biologic-specific inspection readiness (cell bank storage, viral safety documentation, process validation, comparability packages).
questions.rationale: Pre-approval inspection for biologics focuses heavily on cell substrate history, viral safety, process validation, and manufacturing consistency — areas that are unique to biologic GMP and require specific inspection preparation.

questions.question: Is there alignment with ICH guidelines (Q5A-E, Q6B, Q7, Q8-12)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether CMC activities align with ICH guidance. For biologics, the relevant ICH guidelines differ fundamentally: Q5A (viral safety), Q5B (expression constructs), Q5C (stability of biologics), Q5D (cell substrates), Q5E (comparability), Q6B (specifications for biologics) — these replace the SM-focused Q3A/B, Q6A framework.
questions.rationale: The ICH Q5 series is the foundational regulatory framework for biologic CMC. These guidelines address viral safety, cell substrates, stability, comparability, and specifications — concepts that have no parallel in SM development. Non-alignment with Q5 series is a primary cause of regulatory findings for biologics.

questions.question: Are packaging components (e.g., vial, prefilled syringe, autoinjector) qualified?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether packaging and delivery device components are qualified. For biologics, includes protein-surface interaction testing, silicone oil impact assessment, extractables/leachables with protein-containing formulations, and device human factors for self-administration presentations.
questions.rationale: Biologic products are predominantly in vials, prefilled syringes, or autoinjectors — each presenting protein-specific compatibility challenges. Silicone oil from syringes can nucleate protein aggregation. Regulatory pathways for combination products (drug + device) add complexity.

questions.question: Are labelling and serialisation capabilities in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether labelling and serialisation are compliant.
questions.rationale: Proper labelling and serialisation ensure safe use, regulatory compliance, and traceability.

questions.question: Is cold chain validated for the product?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether cold chain is validated end-to-end. For biologics, cold chain is nearly always required (2-8°C for DP, often -20°C or -70°C for DS). Includes qualification of shipping lanes, temperature excursion protocols, and cold chain monitoring for global distribution.
questions.rationale: Virtually all biologics require cold chain — this is not optional as it often is for SM products. Cold chain failures can cause irreversible protein aggregation, potency loss, and particulate formation. Robust cold chain validation is a fundamental commercial requirement for biologics.

questions.question: Are shipping validation and chain-of-custody processes defined?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether shipping is validated with documented chain-of-custody.
questions.rationale: Validated shipping and chain-of-custody are critical for maintaining biologic product quality, particularly given cold chain requirements and protein sensitivity to physical stress (agitation, drops).

questions.question: Is there a Quality Management System (QMS) in place at sponsor/CMO?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether QMS is operational. For biologics, QMS must address biologic-specific quality elements including cell bank management, viral safety monitoring, comparability protocols, and biological reference standard management.
questions.rationale: QMS for biologics must encompass elements unique to cell-based manufacturing. Cell bank management, viral safety, and reference standard (which degrades over time and requires replacement) are biologic-specific quality system requirements.

questions.question: Are CAPA, change control, deviation handling, and batch release procedures active?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether critical quality system processes are operational. For biologics, change control is particularly critical because process changes may require comparability assessment (ICH Q5E) and potentially additional clinical data.
questions.rationale: For biologics, "the process is the product" — any process change can alter the product. Change control for biologics must incorporate comparability impact assessment, which adds complexity beyond standard SM change control.

questions.question: Are internal or external audits conducted regularly?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether audit programmes are active.
questions.rationale: Regular audits verify GMP compliance and demonstrate quality oversight.

questions.question: Is there a documented risk assessment for critical operations?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether formal risk assessments are conducted. For biologics, includes risk assessment of viral safety measures, cell culture contamination controls, and aseptic processing.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific risk assessments must address viral contamination (potentially catastrophic), cell culture microbial contamination, and aseptic processing failures — risks that do not exist in SM manufacturing.

questions.question: Are biosafety and material safety considerations addressed for biologic manufacturing?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether biosafety risk assessments are conducted for cell culture operations, including biosafety level determination, GMO containment requirements, biological waste handling, and personnel protection from biological agents. Assessment of material safety for all raw materials and intermediates.
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing introduces biosafety considerations (working with cell lines, potentially infectious agents, GMOs) that replace the chemical hazard/solvent/OEL focus of SM manufacturing. Biosafety classification and containment requirements must be defined before manufacturing can commence.

questions.question: Are there composition of matter, sequence, and method-of-use patents covering the biologic?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether patent coverage exists for the biologic. For biologics, includes antibody sequence patents, CDR patents, epitope/target patents, Fc engineering patents, bispecific format/platform patents, and method-of-use patents. Assessment of patent landscape including biosimilar entry risks.
questions.rationale: Biologic IP strategy differs fundamentally from SM — composition of matter is defined by amino acid sequence (not chemical structure), and platform patents (e.g., bispecific formats, Fc engineering) create additional FTO considerations. Biosimilar entry risk (after 12-year BPCIA exclusivity) must be assessed.

questions.question: Are there FTO risks related to platform technology, manufacturing processes, or formulation?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether FTO risks are assessed. For biologics, includes FTO analysis of bispecific platform technology (e.g., knobs-into-holes, CrossMab, DVD-Ig), expression system patents, purification process patents, and formulation patents (high-concentration SC formulations, specific buffer/surfactant combinations).
questions.rationale: Biologics face unique FTO challenges around platform technology patents (bispecific formats are heavily patented), expression system patents (CHO cell engineering), and formulation patents. These risks are distinct from SM FTO concerns around synthetic routes and excipients.

questions.question: Is the production cell line fully characterised, banked under GMP, and demonstrated to be stable?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the production cell line (typically CHO) is fully characterised per ICH Q5B and Q5D, including genetic stability assessment over the production cell age limit, identity testing (STR, isoenzyme), karyology, and adventitious agent testing. Assessment of whether master cell bank (MCB) and working cell bank (WCB) are manufactured and stored under GMP conditions with appropriate inventory.
questions.rationale: The cell line is the biological starting material for all biologic manufacturing. Cell line instability can cause loss of productivity, changes in product quality attributes (glycosylation, charge variants), or expression of aberrant proteins. ICH Q5B and Q5D mandate thorough characterisation and stability demonstration. Cell bank loss or contamination can be catastrophic — adequate inventory and dual-site storage are essential risk mitigations.

questions.question: Is the expression construct fully characterised with sequence confirmation and copy number assessment?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the expression construct (vector) is characterised per ICH Q5B, including full sequence of the inserted gene, vector map, regulatory elements, selection markers, integration site analysis (where feasible), copy number, and confirmation that the expressed protein matches the intended sequence (verified by peptide mapping and intact mass).
questions.rationale: The expression construct defines what the cell line produces. Errors in the construct sequence, unexpected rearrangements, or instability of the integration site can lead to production of incorrect or heterogeneous product. ICH Q5B requires detailed construct characterisation as a foundation for product quality assurance.

questions.question: Is there a cell line backup strategy and are cell banks stored at geographically separated sites?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether cell bank inventory is sufficient for the programme lifecycle (clinical through commercial), whether backup cell lines or parallel clones are maintained, and whether cell banks are stored at geographically separated sites to mitigate catastrophic loss.
questions.rationale: Cell bank loss (through contamination, equipment failure, or facility incident) can halt a biologic programme for years. Dual-site storage is industry standard. Backup cell lines provide insurance against primary clone instability or unexpected quality issues. This is a programme-level risk that has no parallel in SM development.

questions.question: For bispecifics: is the cell line strategy appropriate for the bispecific format (single cell vs. separate expression and assembly)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the cell line strategy is fit-for-purpose for the bispecific format. Assessment of whether single-cell co-expression (with engineered heterodimerisation) or separate expression and in vitro assembly is used, and whether the chosen approach delivers acceptable product quality (correct chain pairing, low mispaired species, consistent product profile).
questions.rationale: Bispecific antibodies require correct pairing of two different heavy chains and (often) two different light chains. Mispairing produces inactive or potentially immunogenic species. The cell line strategy (single-cell vs. two-cell) and engineering approach (knobs-into-holes, CrossMab, common light chain) directly determine product quality and manufacturing feasibility. This is a foundational CMC decision unique to bispecifics.

questions.question: Is the primary structure fully confirmed (sequence, post-translational modifications, disulphide bonds)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the amino acid sequence is confirmed by peptide mapping with full coverage, N- and C-terminal sequencing, intact mass, disulphide bond mapping, and characterisation of post-translational modifications (glycosylation, deamidation, oxidation, C-terminal lysine clipping, pyroglutamate formation).
questions.rationale: Biologic drug substances require extensive structural characterisation that has no parallel in SM development. The primary structure — including all post-translational modifications — defines the product and must be fully confirmed. Unexpected modifications can alter potency, stability, immunogenicity, and safety. ICH Q6B mandates comprehensive characterisation.

questions.question: Is the higher-order structure characterised (secondary, tertiary, quaternary)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether higher-order structure is assessed using orthogonal methods: secondary structure (CD, FTIR), tertiary structure (intrinsic fluorescence, near-UV CD, NMR), quaternary structure (SEC-MALS, SV-AUC), and conformation (HDX-MS, limited proteolysis). Assessment of whether higher-order structure is linked to biological activity.
questions.rationale: Higher-order structure determines whether a protein is correctly folded and functionally active. Misfolded proteins may be inactive, aggregate-prone, or immunogenic. Regulatory agencies increasingly expect higher-order structural characterisation, particularly for comparability and biosimilarity assessments. ICH Q6B.

questions.question: Is the glycosylation profile characterised and controlled?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the N-linked and O-linked glycosylation profile is characterised (released glycan analysis by HILIC or CE-LIF, site-specific glycosylation by peptide mapping/LC-MS, sialic acid content, mannose content, fucosylation, galactosylation). Assessment of whether glycosylation is linked to functional activity (e.g., afucosylated species enhancing ADCC) and whether the glycosylation control strategy is defined.
questions.rationale: Glycosylation is the most important post-translational modification for biologic function, stability, PK, and immunogenicity. Afucosylation enhances ADCC; high mannose accelerates clearance; sialylation affects half-life and anti-inflammatory activity. Glycosylation is process-dependent and can shift with cell culture conditions. Control of glycosylation is a critical CMC requirement unique to biologics. ICH Q6B.

questions.question: Is a potency assay developed that reflects the biological mechanism of action?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a potency assay (cell-based bioassay, receptor binding assay, or functional assay) is developed, qualified, and reflects the intended biological activity. For mAbs, includes assessment of target binding, Fc-mediated effector functions (ADCC, CDC, ADCP) where relevant. For bispecifics, includes assessment of the obligate mechanism (simultaneous dual engagement).
questions.rationale: Potency is the defining quality attribute for biologics — it measures biological activity, not just chemical purity. Regulatory agencies require potency assays that reflect the intended MOA. For bispecifics, the potency assay must capture the obligate mechanism (activity dependent on simultaneous dual-target binding). Potency assay development is one of the most challenging and critical CMC activities for biologics. ICH Q6B.

questions.question: Are viral safety studies completed per ICH Q5A (cell substrate testing, process validation, bulk harvest testing)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the three pillars of viral safety per ICH Q5A are addressed: (1) testing of cell substrate and raw materials for adventitious and endogenous viruses; (2) assessment of manufacturing process capability to clear/inactivate viruses (viral clearance validation); and (3) testing of bulk harvest for adventitious viruses. Assessment of study design, virus panel selection, and documentation.
questions.rationale: Viral safety is a critical regulatory requirement unique to biologics manufactured in mammalian cell lines. CHO cells carry endogenous retrovirus-like particles, and adventitious virus contamination (e.g., MVM, reovirus) has caused manufacturing shutdowns. ICH Q5A provides the framework for a comprehensive viral safety strategy. Inadequate viral safety data can result in clinical hold or BLA refusal.

questions.question: Is the viral clearance validation study designed with an appropriate virus panel and conducted to current regulatory standards?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether viral clearance studies use a panel of model viruses representing relevant size, envelope status, and resistance characteristics (typically including MuLV/XMuLV, MVM, Reo-3, PRV). Assessment of whether clearance is demonstrated for both inactivation steps (low pH, detergent) and removal steps (nanofiltration, chromatography), with adequate log reduction values (LRV) and process robustness.
questions.rationale: Viral clearance validation demonstrates that the manufacturing process can remove or inactivate potential viral contaminants. Regulatory agencies expect a comprehensive virus panel, robust LRV values (typically >12-15 log total clearance), and evidence that clearance is maintained under worst-case process conditions. This study is a BLA/MAA requirement with no SM equivalent.

questions.question: Are viral inactivation and removal steps integrated into the manufacturing process?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether dedicated viral inactivation (low pH hold, solvent/detergent treatment) and viral removal (nanofiltration, typically 20nm) steps are incorporated into the downstream purification process. Assessment of whether these steps are validated for process conditions (pH, time, temperature, protein concentration) and are monitored with appropriate IPC.
questions.rationale: Dedicated viral inactivation and removal steps are regulatory requirements for biologic manufacturing in mammalian cell lines. These steps must be specifically designed, validated, and monitored — they cannot be assumed from general purification performance. Regulatory agencies scrutinise these steps intensively during BLA review and pre-approval inspection.

questions.question: Is there a viral contamination response plan and are raw materials assessed for viral risk?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a documented response plan exists for viral contamination events (facility decontamination, root cause investigation, regulatory notification, batch disposition). Assessment of whether raw materials (particularly animal-derived materials, cell culture media) are assessed for viral risk with appropriate controls (irradiation, filtration, sourcing from TSE-free countries).
questions.rationale: Viral contamination events in biologic manufacturing are rare but potentially catastrophic — shutting down facilities for months and destroying product inventory. A documented response plan is essential. Raw material viral risk assessment is a regulatory requirement (ICH Q5A) and a practical necessity for supply chain resilience.

questions.question: Is a comparability strategy defined for assessing the impact of manufacturing process changes on product quality?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a documented comparability strategy exists that defines the analytical, functional, and (if needed) nonclinical/clinical approach for assessing product quality before and after process changes. Assessment of whether the strategy is aligned with ICH Q5E and defines trigger criteria for different levels of comparability assessment.
questions.rationale: For biologics, "the process is the product" — any process change can alter the product's quality attributes, potentially affecting safety and efficacy. ICH Q5E provides the framework for comparability assessment. A prospective comparability strategy is essential because process changes are inevitable during development (scale-up, site transfer, process optimisation) and post-approval (manufacturing improvements, site additions). No equivalent requirement exists for small molecules.

questions.question: Has comparability been demonstrated across process changes made during development?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether analytical comparability (and functional/clinical comparability where warranted) has been demonstrated for all significant process changes. Assessment of statistical approaches used (equivalence testing, quality range-based methods), number of pre- and post-change batches analysed, and attributes compared.
questions.rationale: Regulatory agencies expect comprehensive comparability data whenever the manufacturing process changes. For biologics, this typically involves head-to-head analytical comparison of pre- and post-change batches across all CQAs, supplemented by functional (potency, binding) assessment and potentially PK bridging studies for major changes. Inadequate comparability data can require additional clinical studies or trigger clinical hold.

questions.question: Is the reference standard strategy defined and maintained?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a primary reference standard is established, characterised, qualified, and maintained with a documented replacement strategy. Assessment of whether working reference standards are qualified against the primary standard, and whether stability of the reference standard is monitored.
questions.rationale: Biologic reference standards degrade over time (unlike SM chemical reference standards). A replacement strategy with documented bridging qualification is essential for long-term product lifecycle. The reference standard underpins every potency, identity, and purity measurement — its integrity is critical to product quality assurance.

questions.question: Are post-approval process change and lifecycle management strategies planned?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the programme has a forward-looking strategy for managing post-approval process changes (manufacturing improvements, capacity expansion, site additions) including pre-planned comparability approaches and regulatory filing strategy (prior approval supplement, CBE-30, annual report).
questions.rationale: Post-approval process changes for biologics are more complex and regulatorily burdensome than for SM. Each change requires risk assessment, comparability, and often prior regulatory approval. Forward planning reduces time-to-implementation and regulatory risk. This is a critical lifecycle management consideration unique to biologics.

questions.question: Are single-use systems (SUS) qualified for all product-contact applications (bioreactors, bags, tubing, filters, connectors)?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether SUS components used throughout upstream, downstream, and fill-finish operations are qualified for intended use, including material compatibility with process solutions, functional testing (integrity, gas exchange for bioreactors), and supplier qualification. Assessment of whether component specifications and incoming quality controls are defined.
questions.rationale: Single-use systems are now ubiquitous in biologic manufacturing and present unique qualification challenges that do not exist for traditional stainless steel equipment. Component failures (leaks, tears, lot-to-lot variability) can cause batch loss and contamination. USP <665>/<1665> (effective May 2026) establish new mandatory standards for SUS qualification.

questions.question: Are extractables and leachables (E&L) assessed for all product-contact SUS components per USP <665>/<1665> and BPOG guidelines?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether extractables profiling has been conducted for all product-contact SUS components using standardised protocols (BPOG/BioPhorum, USP <665>). Assessment of whether risk-based leachables evaluation has been performed where extractables data indicate potential risk. Evaluation of whether E&L data are integrated into the overall impurity control strategy and regulatory filing.
questions.rationale: E&L from SUS components can introduce chemical impurities into the biologic product, potentially affecting safety, stability, and quality. USP <665> (mandatory from May 2026) establishes standardised extractables testing requirements for plastic components in biopharmaceutical manufacturing. This is a rapidly evolving regulatory area with increasing scrutiny — programmes without robust E&L data face regulatory risk.

questions.question: Is there a SUS supply chain risk mitigation strategy including component change notification and second sourcing?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether the programme has a strategy for managing SUS supply chain risks, including component change notification agreements with suppliers, assessment of impact of supplier-initiated changes (film composition, gamma irradiation dose, manufacturing process), and qualification of alternative components or suppliers for critical SUS.
questions.rationale: SUS supply chain is fragile — supplier-initiated changes to film composition, sterilisation, or manufacturing can alter E&L profiles and impact product quality. The biologic manufacturer often has limited visibility into these changes. Change notification agreements and second-sourcing strategies are critical risk mitigations increasingly expected by regulators.

questions.question: Is the process validation strategy defined and appropriate for the biologic product lifecycle stage?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a prospective process validation strategy is documented covering Stage 1 (process design/characterisation), Stage 2 (process performance qualification/PPQ), and Stage 3 (continued process verification/CPV). Assessment of whether the strategy addresses all unit operations (upstream, downstream, viral safety steps, fill-finish) and aligns with FDA Process Validation Guidance (2011) and EU Annex 15.
questions.rationale: Process validation for biologics is substantially more complex than for small molecules due to the number of unit operations, the biological variability inherent in cell-based manufacturing, and the criticality of demonstrating consistent product quality. The three-stage lifecycle approach (FDA 2011 guidance) is the regulatory expectation. Validation gaps are a leading cause of BLA Complete Response Letters.

questions.question: Are process characterisation studies completed to define proven acceptable ranges (PARs) and design space for critical process parameters?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether systematic process characterisation (small-scale/DoE studies) has been conducted to understand the relationship between process parameters and CQAs, define PARs for CPPs, and establish design space where applicable. Assessment of whether characterisation studies use scale-down models qualified to represent commercial scale.
questions.rationale: Process characterisation is the scientific foundation for the control strategy and process validation. For biologics, understanding how cell culture parameters (temperature, pH, DO, osmolality, feed timing) affect product quality attributes (glycosylation, charge variants, aggregation) is essential. Scale-down model qualification is a regulatory expectation for justifying parameter ranges. ICH Q8, Q11.

questions.question: Is continued process verification (CPV) / ongoing process verification established with appropriate statistical monitoring?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether a CPV programme is in place to monitor process performance and product quality trends post-validation, using statistical process control (SPC) charts, multivariate data analysis, or equivalent trending approaches. Assessment of whether CPV covers all CQAs and CPPs with defined action and alert limits.
questions.rationale: Stage 3 process validation (CPV) is a regulatory expectation for biologics. Process drift — particularly in cell culture-based processes where biological variability is inherent — must be detected early through statistical monitoring. CPV data also support annual product quality reviews and post-approval change assessments. FDA and EMA increasingly scrutinise CPV programme maturity during inspections.

questions.question: Are hold time and intermediate stability studies completed for all in-process pools and intermediates?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether hold time studies have been conducted for all process intermediates (harvest, capture pool, viral inactivation hold, polishing pools, compounded bulk, filtered bulk) at defined temperatures and durations. Assessment of whether hold conditions are validated and included in the process validation package.
questions.rationale: Biologics manufacturing involves multiple intermediate hold points — each representing a potential quality risk if hold conditions are not validated. Protein degradation (aggregation, deamidation, oxidation), microbial growth, and endotoxin accumulation during holds can compromise product quality. Hold time validation is a regulatory requirement that is frequently cited as a gap in BLA submissions.

questions.question: Are raw materials and critical reagents (media, resins, single-use components) qualified with appropriate incoming testing and change control?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether raw materials used in cell culture (media, feeds, supplements) and purification (chromatography resins, membranes, filters) are qualified with documented specifications, incoming testing, supplier change notification agreements, and risk assessment for animal-derived components. Assessment of whether raw material variability impact on product quality is understood.
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing uses a large number of complex raw materials (cell culture media often containing 50+ components, chromatography resins, nanofiltration membranes) whose variability can directly impact cell growth and product quality. Raw material control maturity is a regulatory focus area and a frequent source of manufacturing deviations and OOS results.

questions.question: Is an extended characterisation package in place covering structure, post-translational modifications, and functional attributes beyond routine release testing?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether an extended characterisation programme exists beyond routine release testing, including: intact/subunit mass spectrometry, peptide mapping with PTM quantitation, disulphide bond mapping, free thiol quantitation, higher-order structure (CD, DSC, HDX-MS), forced degradation pathway characterisation, and functional characterisation (binding kinetics by SPR/BLI, Fc effector function, cell-based potency). Assessment of whether characterisation data supports comparability and regulatory filings.
questions.rationale: Extended characterisation is a regulatory expectation for biologics that goes far beyond release testing. This data supports product understanding, comparability assessment, and the product quality section of the BLA/MAA. Characterisation data must demonstrate comprehensive understanding of the molecule — regulators expect orthogonal methods and complete coverage of all quality attributes. ICH Q6B.

questions.question: Are photostability, freeze-thaw, agitation, and in-use stability studies conducted for the biologic?
questions.rubric_tests: Whether biologic-specific stress conditions are evaluated: photostability (ICH Q1B, particularly important for light-sensitive proteins), freeze-thaw cycling (for frozen DS and DP), agitation/shipping simulation (to assess aggregation propensity during transport), and in-use stability (for multi-dose presentations or reconstituted lyophilised products). Assessment of whether results inform labelling, packaging, and handling instructions.
questions.rationale: Biologics are sensitive to physical stresses (light, freeze-thaw, agitation) that can cause aggregation, particulate formation, and potency loss — degradation pathways not relevant for most small molecules. These studies are critical for defining storage conditions, shipping requirements, and in-use handling instructions. Results directly impact cold chain requirements and patient-facing labelling.
